input.text:
# Feature Requirements: Device Geolocation Spoofing

**Feature ID:** DGS-001  
**Priority:** Medium  
**Status:** Draft  
**Created:** 2026-05-17  
**Owner:** Engineering Team

---

## 1. Overview

### 1.1 Feature Summary
Device geolocation spoofing system that enables users to mock GPS locations on Android devices for testing location-based features, simulate routes, test geofencing, and validate location-dependent functionality without physical movement.

### 1.2 Business Goals
- Enable location-based feature testing without physical travel
- Support geofencing and proximity testing
- Facilitate route simulation for navigation apps
- Enable multi-location testing scenarios
- Reduce testing time for location features by 80%
- Support automated location testing

### 1.3 Success Metrics
- Location spoofing accuracy within 10 meters
- Location update latency < 500ms
- Support 50+ concurrent spoofing sessions
- Route simulation accuracy > 95%
- Support 100+ saved locations

---

## 2. Functional Requirements

### 2.1 Location Spoofing

#### 2.1.1 Mock Location Setup
**REQ-DGS-001:** The system SHALL enable mock location on devices:
- Enable "Allow mock locations" in developer options
- Set app as mock location provider
- Verify mock location permissions
- Handle permission errors gracefully

**REQ-DGS-002:** Mock location SHALL support:
- Latitude and longitude coordinates
- Altitude (meters above sea level)
- Accuracy (meters)
- Bearing (direction in degrees)
- Speed (meters per second)
- Timestamp

**REQ-DGS-003:** The system SHALL validate coordinates:
- Latitude: -90 to 90 degrees
- Longitude: -180 to 180 degrees
- Altitude: -500 to 10,000 meters
- Accuracy: 0 to 1000 meters
- Bearing: 0 to 360 degrees
- Speed: 0 to 100 m/s

**REQ-DGS-004:** Location spoofing SHALL work with:
- Google Maps
- Location-based apps
- Navigation apps
- Geofencing apps
- System location services

#### 2.1.2 Location Input Methods
**REQ-DGS-005:** Users SHALL be able to set location via:
- Map interface (click on map)
- Coordinate input (lat/long)
- Address search (geocoding)
- Saved locations (favorites)
- Current desktop location (if available)
- Import from file (GPX, KML)

**REQ-DGS-006:** Map interface SHALL support:
- Interactive map (Google Maps, OpenStreetMap)
- Zoom and pan
- Search for places
- Display current mock location
- Display location history
- Measure distance between points

**REQ-DGS-007:** Address search SHALL:
- Support full addresses
- Support place names
- Support landmarks
- Support coordinates
- Display search results on map
- Show multiple matches

#### 2.1.3 Location Management
**REQ-DGS-008:** Users SHALL be able to:
- Set mock location
- Update mock location
- Stop mock location (restore real GPS)
- Save location as favorite
- Delete saved locations
- Organize locations in folders

**REQ-DGS-009:** Saved locations SHALL include:
- Name/label
- Coordinates (lat, long, altitude)
- Address (if available)
- Notes/description
- Tags for categorization
- Creation date

**REQ-DGS-010:** The system SHALL provide location presets:
- Major cities worldwide
- Test locations (equator, poles, etc.)
- Common landmarks
- Custom user locations

### 2.2 Route Simulation

#### 2.2.1 Route Creation
**REQ-DGS-011:** Users SHALL be able to create routes:
- Draw route on map (click waypoints)
- Import route from file (GPX, KML)
- Use directions API (start/end points)
- Load saved routes
- Reverse route direction

**REQ-DGS-012:** Route SHALL consist of:
- Multiple waypoints (minimum 2)
- Distance between waypoints
- Total route distance
- Estimated duration
- Route type (driving, walking, cycling)

**REQ-DGS-013:** Route creation SHALL support:
- Add waypoint
- Remove waypoint
- Reorder waypoints
- Edit waypoint coordinates
- Snap to roads (optional)
- Calculate optimal route

#### 2.2.2 Route Playback
**REQ-DGS-014:** Users SHALL be able to play routes:
- Start route playback
- Pause route playback
- Resume route playback
- Stop route playback
- Restart route from beginning
- Jump to specific waypoint

**REQ-DGS-015:** Route playback SHALL support:
- Configurable speed (1x, 2x, 5x, 10x, custom)
- Smooth interpolation between waypoints
- Realistic movement simulation
- Loop route (repeat continuously)
- Reverse playback

**REQ-DGS-016:** During playback, the system SHALL:
- Update location at regular intervals (default 1 second)
- Calculate bearing based on direction
- Calculate speed based on distance/time
- Display current position on map
- Show progress (distance, time, percentage)

#### 2.2.3 Route Management
**REQ-DGS-017:** Users SHALL be able to:
- Save route with name
- Load saved route
- Delete saved route
- Export route (GPX, KML, JSON)
- Share route with other users
- Duplicate route

**REQ-DGS-018:** Route metadata SHALL include:
- Route name
- Description
- Total distance
- Estimated duration
- Number of waypoints
- Route type
- Creation date
- Last used date

### 2.3 Geofencing Testing

#### 2.3.1 Geofence Definition
**REQ-DGS-019:** Users SHALL be able to define geofences:
- Circular geofence (center + radius)
- Polygon geofence (multiple points)
- Named geofence
- Multiple geofences per device

**REQ-DGS-020:** Geofence SHALL include:
- Name/label
- Coordinates (center or polygon points)
- Radius (for circular geofences)
- Trigger type (enter, exit, dwell)
- Dwell time (for dwell triggers)

**REQ-DGS-021:** Geofence visualization SHALL:
- Display on map
- Show geofence boundaries
- Highlight active geofences
- Show current location relative to geofence
- Indicate when inside/outside geofence

#### 2.3.2 Geofence Testing
**REQ-DGS-022:** The system SHALL test geofence triggers:
- Simulate entering geofence
- Simulate exiting geofence
- Simulate dwelling in geofence
- Monitor app response to triggers
- Log geofence events

**REQ-DGS-023:** Geofence testing SHALL:
- Move location to trigger enter event
- Move location to trigger exit event
- Stay in location to trigger dwell event
- Verify app receives geofence events
- Display event notifications

**REQ-DGS-024:** The system SHALL provide geofence scenarios:
- Enter single geofence
- Exit single geofence
- Enter multiple geofences
- Move between geofences
- Rapid enter/exit (edge case)

### 2.4 Location History

#### 2.4.1 History Tracking
**REQ-DGS-025:** The system SHALL track location history:
- Record all mock locations set
- Record route playback positions
- Store timestamps
- Store location metadata
- Limit history to last 1000 locations

**REQ-DGS-026:** Location history SHALL display:
- List of locations with timestamps
- Map view with location trail
- Timeline view
- Statistics (distance traveled, time)

**REQ-DGS-027:** Users SHALL be able to:
- View location history
- Clear location history
- Export location history
- Replay location history
- Filter history by date/time

#### 2.4.2 History Playback
**REQ-DGS-028:** Users SHALL be able to replay history:
- Play recorded location sequence
- Adjust playback speed
- Pause/resume playback
- Jump to specific point in history
- Convert history to route

### 2.5 Multi-device Location

#### 2.5.1 Synchronized Location
**REQ-DGS-029:** Users SHALL be able to set same location on multiple devices:
- Select multiple devices
- Set location on all devices
- Synchronize location updates
- Monitor location on all devices

**REQ-DGS-030:** Synchronized location SHALL:
- Update all devices simultaneously
- Handle device disconnections
- Report success/failure per device
- Display location on all devices

#### 2.5.2 Proximity Testing
**REQ-DGS-031:** The system SHALL support proximity testing:
- Set different locations on different devices
- Calculate distance between devices
- Simulate devices moving closer/farther
- Test proximity-based features
- Visualize device positions on map

**REQ-DGS-032:** Proximity scenarios SHALL include:
- Devices at same location
- Devices within X meters
- Devices moving toward each other
- Devices moving apart
- Devices in different cities/countries

### 2.6 Integration with Test Automation

#### 2.6.1 Programmatic Control
**REQ-DGS-033:** The system SHALL provide API for location control:
- Set location via API
- Start route playback via API
- Stop location spoofing via API
- Get current mock location via API
- Trigger geofence events via API

**REQ-DGS-034:** Test scripts SHALL be able to:
- Set location before test execution
- Change location during test execution
- Verify location-based behavior
- Assert on geofence triggers
- Clean up location after test

#### 2.6.2 Location Assertions
**REQ-DGS-035:** The system SHALL support location assertions:
- Assert current location equals expected
- Assert location within radius of expected
- Assert geofence entered
- Assert geofence exited
- Assert distance traveled

### 2.7 Advanced Features

#### 2.7.1 Location Scenarios
**REQ-DGS-036:** The system SHALL provide pre-built scenarios:
- Commute simulation (home to work)
- Delivery route simulation
- Travel simulation (airport to hotel)
- Border crossing simulation
- Time zone change simulation

**REQ-DGS-037:** Scenarios SHALL be:
- Customizable
- Saveable
- Shareable
- Executable with one click

#### 2.7.2 Location Randomization
**REQ-DGS-038:** The system SHALL support location randomization:
- Random location within radius
- Random location in city/country
- Random route generation
- Useful for stress testing

**REQ-DGS-039:** Randomization SHALL:
- Respect constraints (radius, bounds)
- Generate realistic locations (on roads, in cities)
- Avoid water bodies (optional)
- Generate valid coordinates

---

## 3. Non-Functional Requirements

### 3.1 Performance
**REQ-DGS-NFR-001:** Location update latency SHALL be < 500ms.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-002:** The system SHALL support 50+ concurrent spoofing sessions.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-003:** Route playback SHALL maintain consistent update rate (1 update/second).

**REQ-DGS-NFR-004:** Map interface SHALL respond within 1 second.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-005:** Location spoofing SHALL NOT impact device performance by more than 3% CPU.

### 3.2 Scalability
**REQ-DGS-NFR-006:** The system SHALL scale to support 1000+ devices.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-007:** Database SHALL efficiently store 100,000+ saved locations.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-008:** Route storage SHALL support 10,000+ saved routes.

### 3.3 Reliability
**REQ-DGS-NFR-009:** Location spoofing reliability SHALL be > 99%.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-010:** Route playback SHALL not skip waypoints.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-011:** Location SHALL persist across device reconnections.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-012:** Mock location SHALL be restorable to real GPS.

### 3.4 Security
**REQ-DGS-NFR-013:** All location APIs SHALL require authentication via JWT tokens.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-014:** Location data SHALL be encrypted at rest.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-015:** Mock location SHALL require user confirmation.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-016:** Access to location spoofing SHALL be logged for audit.

### 3.5 Maintainability
**REQ-DGS-NFR-017:** Location service SHALL be implemented as separate microservice in Go.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-018:** Codebase SHALL include unit tests with 80%+ coverage.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-019:** System SHALL expose Prometheus metrics for observability.

**REQ-DGS-NFR-020:** Configuration SHALL be externalized via environment variables.

---

## 4. Integration Requirements

### 4.1 Device Service Integration
**REQ-DGS-INT-001:** Location service SHALL integrate with device-service for device management.

**REQ-DGS-INT-002:** Device-service SHALL provide device connection status.

### 4.2 Test Service Integration
**REQ-DGS-INT-003:** Location service SHALL integrate with test-service for test automation.

**REQ-DGS-INT-004:** Test-service SHALL trigger location changes via API.

### 4.3 API Gateway Integration
**REQ-DGS-INT-005:** All location APIs SHALL be exposed through api-gateway.

**REQ-DGS-INT-006:** API-gateway SHALL implement rate limiting for location endpoints.

### 4.4 Storage Integration
**REQ-DGS-INT-007:** Location service SHALL store locations and routes in PostgreSQL.

**REQ-DGS-INT-008:** Location service SHALL cache current locations in Redis.

---

## 5. User Interface Requirements

### 5.1 Location Control UI
**REQ-DGS-UI-001:** Location control interface SHALL display:
- Interactive map
- Location input controls
- Saved locations list
- Current mock location indicator

### 5.2 Route Management UI
**REQ-DGS-UI-002:** Route management interface SHALL display:
- Route editor (map with waypoints)
- Route list
- Playback controls
- Route statistics

### 5.3 Geofence UI
**REQ-DGS-UI-003:** Geofence interface SHALL display:
- Geofence editor
- Geofence list
- Geofence visualization on map
- Trigger status

---

## 6. Data Requirements

### 6.1 Database Schema

#### 6.1.1 mock_locations Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE mock_locations (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    device_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES devices(id),
    latitude NUMERIC(10, 7) NOT NULL,
    longitude NUMERIC(10, 7) NOT NULL,
    altitude NUMERIC(10, 2),
    accuracy NUMERIC(10, 2),
    bearing NUMERIC(5, 2),
    speed NUMERIC(10, 2),
    timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id)
);
```

#### 6.1.2 saved_locations Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE saved_locations (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    latitude NUMERIC(10, 7) NOT NULL,
    longitude NUMERIC(10, 7) NOT NULL,
    altitude NUMERIC(10, 2),
    address TEXT,
    notes TEXT,
    tags TEXT[],
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 6.1.3 routes Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE routes (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    waypoints JSONB NOT NULL,
    total_distance NUMERIC(10, 2),
    route_type VARCHAR(50),
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 6.1.4 geofences Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE geofences (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    geometry JSONB NOT NULL,
    trigger_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    dwell_time INTEGER,
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

---

## 7. API Requirements

### 7.1 REST API Endpoints

```
POST /api/v1/location/mock
  - Body: { device_id, latitude, longitude, altitude, accuracy }
  - Returns: { success: true }

POST /api/v1/location/stop
  - Body: { device_id }
  - Returns: { success: true }

GET /api/v1/location/current
  - Query params: device_id
  - Returns: Current mock location

POST /api/v1/location/routes
  - Body: { name, waypoints, route_type }
  - Returns: Created route

POST /api/v1/location/routes/{route_id}/play
  - Body: { device_id, speed }
  - Returns: { playback_id, status }

POST /api/v1/location/routes/{route_id}/stop
  - Returns: { success: true }

GET /api/v1/location/saved
  - Returns: Saved locations list

POST /api/v1/location/saved
  - Body: { name, latitude, longitude, address, tags }
  - Returns: Created location

POST /api/v1/location/geofences
  - Body: { name, geometry, trigger_type }
  - Returns: Created geofence

GET /api/v1/location/history
  - Query params: device_id, start_time, end_time
  - Returns: Location history
```

---

## 8. Acceptance Criteria

### 8.1 Feature Complete Criteria
- [ ] All functional requirements implemented
- [ ] All non-functional requirements met
- [ ] UI components implemented
- [ ] Database schema deployed
- [ ] API documentation completed
- [ ] Unit tests achieve 80%+ coverage
- [ ] Integration tests cover critical flows
- [ ] Performance benchmarks met

### 8.2 User Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User can set mock location on device
- [ ] Location displayed on map
- [ ] Route playback working smoothly
- [ ] Geofence testing working
- [ ] Multi-device location working
- [ ] Integration with test automation working
- [ ] Location accuracy within 10 meters

---

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|

input.text:
# Feature Requirements: App Performance Profiler

**Feature ID:** APP-001  
**Priority:** Medium  
**Status:** Draft  
**Created:** 2026-05-17  
**Owner:** Engineering Team

---

## 1. Overview

### 1.1 Feature Summary
App-level performance profiling system that monitors and analyzes Android application performance metrics including CPU usage, memory consumption, frame rate, network activity, battery impact, and detects performance issues like ANRs, crashes, and memory leaks.

### 1.2 Business Goals
- Enable deep app performance analysis
- Identify performance bottlenecks and optimization opportunities
- Detect memory leaks and resource issues
- Monitor app stability (crashes, ANRs)
- Improve app quality and user experience
- Reduce performance debugging time by 70%

### 1.3 Success Metrics
- Profile 100+ apps simultaneously
- Real-time metrics update latency < 1 second
- Memory leak detection accuracy > 90%
- ANR/crash detection rate 100%
- Performance report generation < 5 seconds

---

## 2. Functional Requirements

### 2.1 CPU Profiling

#### 2.1.1 CPU Metrics Collection
**REQ-APP-001:** The system SHALL collect app-level CPU metrics:
- Total CPU usage (percentage)
- Per-thread CPU usage
- CPU time (user, system, idle)
- CPU frequency
- Thread count
- Thread states (running, sleeping, waiting)

**REQ-APP-002:** CPU profiling SHALL support:
- Real-time monitoring
- Historical data tracking
- Per-method profiling (with instrumentation)
- Call stack sampling
- Hot spot detection

**REQ-APP-003:** The system SHALL detect CPU issues:
- High CPU usage (> 80% sustained)
- CPU spikes
- Busy threads
- Inefficient algorithms
- Blocking operations on main thread

**REQ-APP-004:** CPU profiling SHALL use:
- Android Profiler API
- ADB commands (dumpsys cpuinfo)
- Simpleperf (for detailed profiling)
- Method tracing (optional)

#### 2.1.2 Thread Analysis
**REQ-APP-005:** The system SHALL analyze threads:
- List all app threads
- Thread CPU usage
- Thread state over time
- Thread creation/destruction
- Thread pool utilization

**REQ-APP-006:** Thread analysis SHALL identify:
- Main thread blocking
- Thread leaks
- Excessive thread creation
- Deadlocks
- Thread contention

### 2.2 Memory Profiling

#### 2.2.1 Memory Metrics Collection
**REQ-APP-007:** The system SHALL collect memory metrics:
- Total memory usage (MB)
- Heap memory (allocated, free)
- Native memory
- Graphics memory
- Memory by type (Java, Native, Graphics, Stack, Code)
- Garbage collection events
- Memory allocations per second

**REQ-APP-008:** Memory profiling SHALL support:
- Real-time monitoring
- Historical data tracking
- Heap dump capture
- Allocation tracking
- Object retention analysis

**REQ-APP-009:** The system SHALL detect memory issues:
- Memory leaks
- High memory usage (> 80% of limit)
- Memory growth over time
- Excessive allocations
- Large object allocations
- Frequent garbage collections

**REQ-APP-010:** Memory profiling SHALL use:
- Android Profiler API
- ADB commands (dumpsys meminfo)
- HPROF heap dumps
- Allocation tracking

#### 2.2.2 Memory Leak Detection
**REQ-APP-011:** The system SHALL detect memory leaks:
- Analyze heap dumps
- Identify retained objects
- Find leak suspects
- Calculate retained size
- Trace reference chains

**REQ-APP-012:** Leak detection SHALL:
- Run automatically at intervals
- Compare heap dumps over time
- Identify growing object counts
- Highlight leak suspects
- Provide fix suggestions

**REQ-APP-013:** Leak reports SHALL include:
- Leaked object type
- Retained size
- Reference chain (GC root to object)
- Allocation stack trace (if available)
- Suggested fixes

### 2.3 Frame Rate Monitoring

#### 2.3.1 FPS Metrics Collection
**REQ-APP-014:** The system SHALL collect frame rate metrics:
- Current FPS (frames per second)
- Average FPS over time
- Frame time (ms per frame)
- Jank count (dropped frames)
- Slow frames (> 16ms)
- Frozen frames (> 700ms)

**REQ-APP-015:** Frame rate monitoring SHALL:
- Track UI rendering performance
- Detect frame drops
- Identify jank patterns
- Measure smoothness
- Compare against 60 FPS target

**REQ-APP-016:** The system SHALL detect rendering issues:
- Low FPS (< 30 FPS sustained)
- Frame drops (> 5% frames dropped)
- Jank (inconsistent frame times)
- Slow rendering (> 16ms per frame)
- UI freezes (> 700ms frame time)

**REQ-APP-017:** Frame rate profiling SHALL use:
- Android FrameMetrics API
- SurfaceFlinger data
- Choreographer callbacks
- GPU rendering profiling

#### 2.3.2 Rendering Analysis
**REQ-APP-018:** The system SHALL analyze rendering:
- Overdraw detection
- View hierarchy depth
- Layout complexity
- Expensive operations on UI thread
- GPU rendering time

**REQ-APP-019:** Rendering analysis SHALL identify:
- Overdraw hotspots
- Complex layouts
- Inefficient view hierarchies
- Expensive draw operations
- GPU bottlenecks

### 2.4 Network Profiling

#### 2.4.1 Network Metrics Collection
**REQ-APP-020:** The system SHALL collect network metrics:
- Total data transferred (upload/download)
- Network requests count
- Request duration
- Request size
- Response size
- Connection type (WiFi, cellular)
- Network errors

**REQ-APP-021:** Network profiling SHALL:
- Track all network activity
- Measure bandwidth usage
- Identify slow requests
- Detect network errors
- Monitor connection quality

**REQ-APP-022:** The system SHALL detect network issues:
- Excessive data usage
- Slow network requests (> 3 seconds)
- Failed requests (> 10% error rate)
- Large payloads (> 1MB)
- Frequent requests (> 100/minute)

**REQ-APP-023:** Network profiling SHALL integrate with:
- Network Traffic Monitor (if available)
- Android NetworkStats API
- Traffic stats

### 2.5 Battery Impact Analysis

#### 2.5.1 Battery Metrics Collection
**REQ-APP-024:** The system SHALL collect battery metrics:
- Battery drain rate (% per hour)
- Power consumption by component (CPU, network, GPS, screen)
- Wake locks held
- Alarms triggered
- Background activity

**REQ-APP-025:** Battery profiling SHALL:
- Measure app battery impact
- Compare with other apps
- Track battery drain over time
- Identify power-hungry operations

**REQ-APP-026:** The system SHALL detect battery issues:
- High battery drain (> 10% per hour)
- Wake lock abuse
- Excessive background activity
- Frequent alarms
- GPS overuse

**REQ-APP-027:** Battery profiling SHALL use:
- Android BatteryStats
- PowerProfile data
- Wake lock tracking
- Alarm manager stats

### 2.6 Crash and ANR Detection

#### 2.6.1 Crash Detection
**REQ-APP-028:** The system SHALL detect app crashes:
- Monitor for crash events
- Capture crash logs (logcat)
- Extract stack traces
- Identify crash type (exception, native crash, etc.)
- Record crash context (activity, memory, etc.)

**REQ-APP-029:** Crash detection SHALL:
- Detect crashes in real-time
- Capture crash immediately after occurrence
- Store crash reports
- Notify user of crash
- Associate crash with test execution (if applicable)

**REQ-APP-030:** Crash reports SHALL include:
- Exception type and message
- Stack trace
- Thread information
- Memory state at crash
- Device state
- App version
- Timestamp

#### 2.6.2 ANR Detection
**REQ-APP-031:** The system SHALL detect ANRs (Application Not Responding):
- Monitor for ANR events
- Capture ANR traces
- Identify blocking operations
- Record ANR context

**REQ-APP-032:** ANR detection SHALL:
- Detect ANRs in real-time
- Capture ANR trace immediately
- Identify root cause (blocking operation)
- Store ANR reports

**REQ-APP-033:** ANR reports SHALL include:
- Main thread stack trace
- All thread stack traces
- Blocking operation details
- CPU state
- Memory state
- Timestamp

### 2.7 Performance Reporting

#### 2.7.1 Real-time Dashboard
**REQ-APP-034:** The system SHALL provide real-time dashboard:
- Current CPU usage
- Current memory usage
- Current FPS
- Network activity
- Battery drain rate
- Active issues count

**REQ-APP-035:** Dashboard SHALL:
- Update in real-time (< 1 second latency)
- Display charts and graphs
- Highlight issues
- Show trends
- Support multiple apps simultaneously

#### 2.7.2 Historical Analysis
**REQ-APP-036:** The system SHALL provide historical analysis:
- Performance metrics over time
- Trend analysis
- Comparison between sessions
- Baseline comparison
- Regression detection

**REQ-APP-037:** Historical data SHALL:
- Be stored for 90 days
- Support time range selection
- Support metric aggregation
- Support export

#### 2.7.3 Performance Reports
**REQ-APP-038:** The system SHALL generate performance reports:
- Summary report (overview of all metrics)
- Detailed report (deep dive into specific metrics)
- Issue report (list of detected issues)
- Comparison report (compare sessions)

**REQ-APP-039:** Reports SHALL include:
- Performance metrics summary
- Charts and graphs
- Issue list with severity
- Recommendations
- Export options (PDF, HTML, JSON)

**REQ-APP-040:** The system SHALL provide recommendations:
- Optimization suggestions
- Best practices
- Fix suggestions for detected issues
- Priority ranking

### 2.8 Profiling Sessions

#### 2.8.1 Session Management
**REQ-APP-041:** Users SHALL be able to:
- Start profiling session
- Stop profiling session
- Pause/resume session
- Save session
- Load saved session
- Compare sessions

**REQ-APP-042:** Profiling session SHALL include:
- Session name
- App being profiled
- Device information
- Start/end time
- Duration
- Collected metrics
- Detected issues

**REQ-APP-043:** Session configuration SHALL allow:
- Select metrics to collect
- Set sampling rate
- Enable/disable specific profilers
- Set alert thresholds
- Configure data retention

#### 2.8.2 Baseline Comparison
**REQ-APP-044:** The system SHALL support baseline comparison:
- Set session as baseline
- Compare current session with baseline
- Detect regressions
- Highlight improvements
- Show delta metrics

**REQ-APP-045:** Regression detection SHALL identify:
- Performance degradation (slower)
- Memory increase
- FPS decrease
- Battery drain increase
- New crashes/ANRs

### 2.9 Integration with Test Automation

#### 2.9.1 Automated Profiling
**REQ-APP-046:** The system SHALL integrate with test automation:
- Start profiling before test execution
- Stop profiling after test execution
- Associate profile with test results
- Validate performance in tests
- Fail tests on performance regression

**REQ-APP-047:** Performance assertions SHALL support:
- Assert CPU usage < threshold
- Assert memory usage < threshold
- Assert FPS > threshold
- Assert no memory leaks
- Assert no crashes/ANRs

#### 2.9.2 Continuous Performance Testing
**REQ-APP-048:** The system SHALL support continuous testing:
- Profile on every test run
- Track performance over time
- Detect regressions automatically
- Alert on performance degradation
- Generate trend reports

---

## 3. Non-Functional Requirements

### 3.1 Performance
**REQ-APP-NFR-001:** Profiling SHALL NOT impact app performance by more than 5%.

**REQ-APP-NFR-002:** Real-time metrics update latency SHALL be < 1 second.

**REQ-APP-NFR-003:** The system SHALL support 100+ concurrent profiling sessions.

**REQ-APP-NFR-004:** Memory leak detection SHALL complete within 30 seconds.

**REQ-APP-NFR-005:** Performance report generation SHALL complete within 5 seconds.

### 3.2 Scalability
**REQ-APP-NFR-006:** The system SHALL scale to support 1000+ devices.

**REQ-APP-NFR-007:** Database SHALL efficiently store 1M+ profiling sessions.

**REQ-APP-NFR-008:** Historical data SHALL be queryable for 90 days.

### 3.3 Reliability
**REQ-APP-NFR-009:** Profiling service SHALL have 99.5% uptime.

**REQ-APP-NFR-010:** Crash/ANR detection rate SHALL be 100%.

**REQ-APP-NFR-011:** Memory leak detection accuracy SHALL be > 90%.

**REQ-APP-NFR-012:** Profiling data SHALL be persisted every 5 minutes.

### 3.4 Security
**REQ-APP-NFR-013:** All profiling APIs SHALL require authentication via JWT tokens.

**REQ-APP-NFR-014:** Profiling data SHALL be encrypted at rest.

**REQ-APP-NFR-015:** Heap dumps SHALL be encrypted and access-controlled.

**REQ-APP-NFR-016:** Access to profiling data SHALL be logged for audit.

### 3.5 Maintainability
**REQ-APP-NFR-017:** Profiling service SHALL be implemented as separate microservice in Go.

**REQ-APP-NFR-018:** Codebase SHALL include unit tests with 80%+ coverage.

**REQ-APP-NFR-019:** System SHALL expose Prometheus metrics for observability.

**REQ-APP-NFR-020:** Configuration SHALL be externalized via environment variables.

---

## 4. Integration Requirements

### 4.1 Device Service Integration
**REQ-APP-INT-001:** Profiling service SHALL integrate with device-service for device management.

**REQ-APP-INT-002:** Device-service SHALL provide device connection status.

### 4.2 Performance Monitoring Integration
**REQ-APP-INT-003:** Profiling service SHALL integrate with device performance monitoring.

**REQ-APP-INT-004:** Share device-level metrics with app-level profiling.

### 4.3 Test Service Integration
**REQ-APP-INT-005:** Profiling service SHALL integrate with test-service for automated profiling.

**REQ-APP-INT-006:** Test-service SHALL trigger profiling via API.

### 4.4 API Gateway Integration
**REQ-APP-INT-007:** All profiling APIs SHALL be exposed through api-gateway.

**REQ-APP-INT-008:** API-gateway SHALL implement rate limiting for profiling endpoints.

### 4.5 Storage Integration
**REQ-APP-INT-009:** Profiling service SHALL store data in PostgreSQL.

**REQ-APP-INT-010:** Profiling service SHALL cache recent data in Redis.

**REQ-APP-INT-011:** Profiling service SHALL store heap dumps in S3/MinIO.

---

## 5. User Interface Requirements

### 5.1 Profiling Dashboard UI
**REQ-APP-UI-001:** Dashboard SHALL display:
- Real-time metrics (CPU, memory, FPS, network, battery)
- Charts and graphs
- Issue alerts
- Session controls

### 5.2 Analysis UI
**REQ-APP-UI-002:** Analysis interface SHALL display:
- Historical metrics
- Trend analysis
- Issue details
- Recommendations

### 5.3 Report UI
**REQ-APP-UI-003:** Report interface SHALL display:
- Performance summary
- Detailed metrics
- Issue list
- Export options

---

## 6. Data Requirements

### 6.1 Database Schema

#### 6.1.1 profiling_sessions Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE profiling_sessions (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    device_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES devices(id),
    app_package VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    session_name VARCHAR(255),
    started_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    ended_at TIMESTAMP,
    duration_seconds INTEGER,
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id)
);
```

#### 6.1.2 performance_metrics Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE performance_metrics (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    session_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES profiling_sessions(id),
    metric_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    metric_value NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,
    timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
) PARTITION BY RANGE (timestamp);
```

#### 6.1.3 detected_issues Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE detected_issues (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    session_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES profiling_sessions(id),
    issue_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    severity VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    details JSONB,
    detected_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
);
```

---

## 7. API Requirements

### 7.1 REST API Endpoints

```
POST /api/v1/profiling/sessions/start
  - Body: { device_id, app_package, config }
  - Returns: { session_id, status }

POST /api/v1/profiling/sessions/{session_id}/stop
  - Returns: { session_id, status }

GET /api/v1/profiling/sessions/{session_id}/metrics
  - Query params: metric_type, start_time, end_time
  - Returns: Metrics data

GET /api/v1/profiling/sessions/{session_id}/issues
  - Returns: Detected issues

POST /api/v1/profiling/sessions/{session_id}/heap-dump
  - Returns: { heap_dump_id, status }

GET /api/v1/profiling/sessions/{session_id}/report
  - Query params: format
  - Returns: Performance report

POST /api/v1/profiling/sessions/{session_id}/compare
  - Body: { baseline_session_id }
  - Returns: Comparison report
```

---

## 8. Acceptance Criteria

### 8.1 Feature Complete Criteria
- [ ] All functional requirements implemented
- [ ] All non-functional requirements met
- [ ] UI components implemented
- [ ] Database schema deployed
- [ ] API documentation completed
- [ ] Unit tests achieve 80%+ coverage
- [ ] Integration tests cover critical flows
- [ ] Performance benchmarks met

### 8.2 User Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User can profile app performance
- [ ] Real-time metrics displayed
- [ ] Memory leaks detected
- [ ] Crashes and ANRs detected
- [ ] Performance reports generated
- [ ] Integration with test automation working
- [ ] Profiling overhead < 5%

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|

input.text:
# Feature Requirements: Scheduled Task Automation

**Feature ID:** STA-001  
**Priority:** Medium  
**Status:** Draft  
**Created:** 2026-05-17  
**Owner:** Engineering Team

---

## 1. Overview

### 1.1 Feature Summary
Scheduled task automation system that enables users to schedule and automate recurring tasks on Android devices including test execution, file operations, performance monitoring, and custom workflows with support for cron-like scheduling, task chaining, conditional execution, and comprehensive task management.

### 1.2 Business Goals
- Enable automated recurring testing without manual intervention
- Support scheduled maintenance tasks (cleanup, updates, etc.)
- Facilitate continuous testing and monitoring
- Reduce manual task execution effort by 90%
- Enable 24/7 automated operations
- Support complex workflow automation

### 1.3 Success Metrics
- Schedule 1000+ tasks across devices
- Task execution accuracy > 99.9%
- Task execution latency < 5 seconds from scheduled time
- Support 100+ concurrent task executions
- Task success rate > 95%

---

## 2. Functional Requirements

### 2.1 Task Scheduling

#### 2.1.1 Schedule Types
**REQ-STA-001:** The system SHALL support multiple schedule types:
- **One-time:** Execute once at specific date/time
- **Recurring:** Execute at regular intervals
- **Cron:** Execute based on cron expression
- **Event-triggered:** Execute on specific events
- **Manual:** Execute on-demand

**REQ-STA-002:** Recurring schedules SHALL support:
- Every X minutes/hours/days/weeks/months
- Daily at specific time
- Weekly on specific days
- Monthly on specific date
- Custom intervals

**REQ-STA-003:** Cron expressions SHALL support:
- Standard cron syntax (minute, hour, day, month, weekday)
- Extended syntax (seconds, year)
- Special characters (*, -, /, ?)
- Named values (MON, TUE, JAN, FEB, etc.)
- Validation and preview

**REQ-STA-004:** Event-triggered schedules SHALL support:
- Device connected/disconnected
- Test completed
- Performance threshold exceeded
- File uploaded/downloaded
- Custom events

#### 2.1.2 Schedule Configuration
**REQ-STA-005:** Users SHALL be able to configure:
- Schedule name and description
- Schedule type
- Start date/time
- End date/time (optional)
- Time zone
- Enabled/disabled state

**REQ-STA-006:** Schedule SHALL support:
- Immediate execution (run now)
- Delayed start (start after X minutes)
- Expiration (stop after date/time)
- Max executions (stop after X runs)
- Skip if previous execution still running

**REQ-STA-007:** The system SHALL validate schedules:
- Check for valid cron expressions
- Verify start time is in future
- Verify end time is after start time
- Check for conflicts
- Warn about frequent executions

### 2.2 Task Types

#### 2.2.1 Test Execution Tasks
**REQ-STA-008:** The system SHALL support test execution tasks:
- Run specific test script
- Run test suite
- Run all tests for app
- Run tests with parameters
- Run tests on multiple devices

**REQ-STA-009:** Test execution tasks SHALL:
- Select test script/suite
- Configure test parameters
- Select target devices
- Set timeout
- Configure retry policy
- Collect test results

#### 2.2.2 File Operation Tasks
**REQ-STA-010:** The system SHALL support file operation tasks:
- Upload files to devices
- Download files from devices
- Delete files
- Sync files between devices
- Cleanup old files
- Backup files

**REQ-STA-011:** File operation tasks SHALL:
- Specify source and destination
- Support wildcards and patterns
- Handle errors gracefully
- Report operation results

#### 2.2.3 Performance Monitoring Tasks
**REQ-STA-012:** The system SHALL support monitoring tasks:
- Start performance monitoring
- Stop performance monitoring
- Capture performance snapshot
- Generate performance report
- Check performance thresholds

**REQ-STA-013:** Monitoring tasks SHALL:
- Configure metrics to collect
- Set alert thresholds
- Specify report format
- Define notification recipients

#### 2.2.4 Device Control Tasks
**REQ-STA-014:** The system SHALL support device control tasks:
- Restart device
- Clear app data
- Install/uninstall app
- Enable/disable features
- Change device settings
- Execute shell commands

**REQ-STA-015:** Device control tasks SHALL:
- Require confirmation for destructive operations
- Support multiple devices
- Handle device unavailability
- Log all operations

#### 2.2.5 Custom Tasks
**REQ-STA-016:** The system SHALL support custom tasks:
- Execute custom scripts (Python, JavaScript)
- Call external APIs
- Run custom commands
- Chain multiple operations

**REQ-STA-017:** Custom tasks SHALL:
- Support parameters
- Access task context
- Return results
- Handle errors

### 2.3 Task Chaining

#### 2.3.1 Sequential Execution
**REQ-STA-018:** Users SHALL be able to chain tasks:
- Execute tasks in sequence
- Pass output from one task to next
- Stop chain on failure (optional)
- Continue chain on failure (optional)

**REQ-STA-019:** Task chains SHALL support:
- Multiple tasks (unlimited)
- Task dependencies
- Conditional execution
- Parallel execution (for independent tasks)

**REQ-STA-020:** Task output SHALL be:
- Accessible to subsequent tasks
- Stored in task context
- Available for conditions
- Logged for debugging

#### 2.3.2 Conditional Execution
**REQ-STA-021:** The system SHALL support conditional execution:
- Execute task if condition is true
- Skip task if condition is false
- Conditions based on previous task results
- Conditions based on device state
- Conditions based on time/date

**REQ-STA-022:** Conditions SHALL support:
- Comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=)
- Logical operators (AND, OR, NOT)
- String operations (contains, starts with, ends with)
- Numeric operations
- Boolean operations

**REQ-STA-023:** Condition examples:
- If test failed, send notification
- If memory usage > 80%, restart app
- If file exists, skip upload
- If weekday, run tests
- If device online, execute task

#### 2.3.3 Error Handling
**REQ-STA-024:** Task chains SHALL support error handling:
- Retry failed tasks (configurable attempts)
- Fallback tasks on failure
- Error notifications
- Continue or abort chain on error

**REQ-STA-025:** Retry policy SHALL include:
- Max retry attempts
- Retry delay (fixed, exponential backoff)
- Retry conditions (which errors to retry)

### 2.4 Task Execution

#### 2.4.1 Execution Control
**REQ-STA-026:** Users SHALL be able to:
- Start task execution manually
- Stop running task
- Pause scheduled task
- Resume paused task
- Skip next execution

**REQ-STA-027:** Task execution SHALL:
- Start at scheduled time (within 5 seconds)
- Execute on correct device(s)
- Respect timeout settings
- Handle device disconnections
- Log all operations

**REQ-STA-028:** The system SHALL handle conflicts:
- Detect overlapping executions
- Queue or skip based on configuration
- Warn about frequent schedules
- Prevent duplicate executions

#### 2.4.2 Execution Monitoring
**REQ-STA-029:** Users SHALL be able to monitor execution:
- View currently running tasks
- View task progress
- View task logs in real-time
- View execution history
- View execution statistics

**REQ-STA-030:** Execution status SHALL include:
- Pending (scheduled, not yet started)
- Running (currently executing)
- Completed (finished successfully)
- Failed (finished with errors)
- Cancelled (stopped by user)
- Skipped (skipped due to condition)

**REQ-STA-031:** Execution details SHALL include:
- Start time
- End time
- Duration
- Status
- Result/output
- Error message (if failed)
- Device information

#### 2.4.3 Execution History
**REQ-STA-032:** The system SHALL maintain execution history:
- Store all task executions
- Retain history for 90 days
- Support filtering and search
- Support export
- Display statistics

**REQ-STA-033:** Execution history SHALL show:
- Execution timeline
- Success/failure rate
- Average duration
- Trend analysis
- Failure patterns

### 2.5 Task Management

#### 2.5.1 Task Organization
**REQ-STA-034:** Users SHALL be able to organize tasks:
- Create task folders/groups
- Tag tasks for categorization
- Search tasks by name/tag
- Filter tasks by type/status
- Sort tasks by various criteria

**REQ-STA-035:** Task metadata SHALL include:
- Task name and description
- Task type
- Schedule configuration
- Target devices
- Created by and date
- Last modified date
- Last execution date
- Next execution date

#### 2.5.2 Task Templates
**REQ-STA-036:** The system SHALL provide task templates:
- Pre-configured common tasks
- Customizable templates
- User-created templates
- Shareable templates

**REQ-STA-037:** Template examples:
- Daily test execution
- Weekly performance report
- Nightly cleanup
- Hourly health check
- Monthly backup

**REQ-STA-038:** Users SHALL be able to:
- Create task from template
- Save task as template
- Edit templates
- Delete templates
- Share templates with team

#### 2.5.3 Task Import/Export
**REQ-STA-039:** The system SHALL support task import/export:
- Export tasks as JSON/YAML
- Import tasks from file
- Bulk export/import
- Validate imported tasks

**REQ-STA-040:** Export SHALL include:
- Task configuration
- Schedule settings
- Task chains
- Conditions
- Metadata

### 2.6 Notifications

#### 2.6.1 Notification Types
**REQ-STA-041:** The system SHALL send notifications for:
- Task execution started
- Task execution completed
- Task execution failed
- Task execution skipped
- Schedule enabled/disabled
- Task approaching expiration

**REQ-STA-042:** Notification channels SHALL include:
- In-app notifications
- Desktop notifications (Tauri)
- Email notifications (future)
- Webhook notifications (future)

**REQ-STA-043:** Notification configuration SHALL allow:
- Enable/disable per task
- Select notification channels
- Configure notification conditions
- Customize notification content

#### 2.6.2 Alerting
**REQ-STA-044:** The system SHALL support alerting:
- Alert on consecutive failures (X failures in a row)
- Alert on failure rate (> X% failures)
- Alert on execution duration (> X minutes)
- Alert on missed executions

**REQ-STA-045:** Alerts SHALL:
- Have severity levels (info, warning, critical)
- Be configurable per task
- Support escalation
- Be logged for audit

### 2.7 Task Dependencies

#### 2.7.1 Dependency Management
**REQ-STA-046:** Tasks SHALL support dependencies:
- Task A must complete before Task B starts
- Task B waits for Task A completion
- Task B skips if Task A fails
- Multiple dependencies (Task C depends on A and B)

**REQ-STA-047:** Dependency resolution SHALL:
- Detect circular dependencies
- Validate dependency graph
- Execute tasks in correct order
- Handle dependency failures

#### 2.7.2 Resource Dependencies
**REQ-STA-048:** Tasks SHALL support resource dependencies:
- Device availability
- File existence
- Service availability
- Network connectivity

**REQ-STA-049:** Resource checks SHALL:
- Verify before execution
- Wait for resource (with timeout)
- Skip or fail if unavailable
- Retry on resource availability

### 2.8 Integration with Other Features

#### 2.8.1 Test Automation Integration
**REQ-STA-050:** Scheduled tasks SHALL integrate with test automation:
- Schedule test execution
- Pass test parameters
- Collect test results
- Trigger on test events

#### 2.8.2 Performance Monitoring Integration
**REQ-STA-051:** Scheduled tasks SHALL integrate with performance monitoring:
- Schedule performance profiling
- Collect performance metrics
- Generate performance reports
- Alert on performance issues

#### 2.8.3 File Manager Integration
**REQ-STA-052:** Scheduled tasks SHALL integrate with file manager:
- Schedule file operations
- Sync files on schedule
- Cleanup files periodically
- Backup files automatically

---

## 3. Non-Functional Requirements

### 3.1 Performance
**REQ-STA-NFR-001:** Task execution latency SHALL be < 5 seconds from scheduled time.

**REQ-STA-NFR-002:** The system SHALL support 100+ concurrent task executions.

**REQ-STA-NFR-003:** Schedule evaluation SHALL complete within 1 second.

**REQ-STA-NFR-004:** Task history queries SHALL return within 2 seconds for 10,000+ executions.

**REQ-STA-NFR-005:** Task execution SHALL NOT impact device performance by more than 5% CPU.

### 3.2 Scalability
**REQ-STA-NFR-006:** The system SHALL scale to support 10,000+ scheduled tasks.

**REQ-STA-NFR-007:** Database SHALL efficiently store 1M+ task executions.

**REQ-STA-NFR-008:** Scheduler SHALL handle 1000+ tasks per minute.

### 3.3 Reliability
**REQ-STA-NFR-009:** Task execution accuracy SHALL be > 99.9%.

**REQ-STA-NFR-010:** Scheduler SHALL have 99.9% uptime.

**REQ-STA-NFR-011:** Failed tasks SHALL be automatically retried based on policy.

**REQ-STA-NFR-012:** Task state SHALL be persisted every 1 minute.

### 3.4 Security
**REQ-STA-NFR-013:** All task APIs SHALL require authentication via JWT tokens.

**REQ-STA-NFR-014:** Task configurations SHALL be encrypted at rest.

**REQ-STA-NFR-015:** Task execution SHALL respect user permissions.

**REQ-STA-NFR-016:** All task operations SHALL be logged for audit.

### 3.5 Maintainability
**REQ-STA-NFR-017:** Scheduler service SHALL be implemented as separate microservice in Go.

**REQ-STA-NFR-018:** Codebase SHALL include unit tests with 80%+ coverage.

**REQ-STA-NFR-019:** System SHALL expose Prometheus metrics for observability.

**REQ-STA-NFR-020:** Configuration SHALL be externalized via environment variables.

---

## 4. Integration Requirements

### 4.1 Device Service Integration
**REQ-STA-INT-001:** Scheduler service SHALL integrate with device-service for device management.

**REQ-STA-INT-002:** Device-service SHALL provide device availability status.

### 4.2 Test Service Integration
**REQ-STA-INT-003:** Scheduler service SHALL integrate with test-service for test execution.

**REQ-STA-INT-004:** Test-service SHALL execute tests triggered by scheduler.

### 4.3 File Service Integration
**REQ-STA-INT-005:** Scheduler service SHALL integrate with file-service for file operations.

**REQ-STA-INT-006:** File-service SHALL execute file operations triggered by scheduler.

### 4.4 API Gateway Integration
**REQ-STA-INT-007:** All scheduler APIs SHALL be exposed through api-gateway.

**REQ-STA-INT-008:** API-gateway SHALL implement rate limiting for scheduler endpoints.

### 4.5 Storage Integration
**REQ-STA-INT-009:** Scheduler service SHALL store tasks and executions in PostgreSQL.

**REQ-STA-INT-010:** Scheduler service SHALL cache active schedules in Redis.

---

## 5. User Interface Requirements

### 5.1 Task Management UI
**REQ-STA-UI-001:** Task management interface SHALL display:
- Task list with status
- Task creation/edit form
- Schedule configuration
- Task execution history

### 5.2 Execution Monitor UI
**REQ-STA-UI-002:** Execution monitor SHALL display:
- Currently running tasks
- Execution progress
- Real-time logs
- Execution statistics

### 5.3 Calendar View UI
**REQ-STA-UI-003:** Calendar view SHALL display:
- Scheduled tasks on calendar
- Execution history
- Upcoming executions
- Task timeline

---

## 6. Data Requirements

### 6.1 Database Schema

#### 6.1.1 scheduled_tasks Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE scheduled_tasks (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    task_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    task_config JSONB NOT NULL,
    schedule_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    schedule_config JSONB NOT NULL,
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
    next_execution TIMESTAMP,
    last_execution TIMESTAMP,
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 6.1.2 task_executions Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE task_executions (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    task_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES scheduled_tasks(id),
    device_id UUID REFERENCES devices(id),
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    started_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    ended_at TIMESTAMP,
    duration_seconds INTEGER,
    result JSONB,
    error_message TEXT,
    logs TEXT
);
```

#### 6.1.3 task_chains Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE task_chains (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    tasks JSONB NOT NULL,
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

---

## 7. API Requirements

### 7.1 REST API Endpoints

```
POST /api/v1/scheduler/tasks
  - Body: { name, task_type, task_config, schedule_config }
  - Returns: Created task

GET /api/v1/scheduler/tasks
  - Query params: filter, limit, offset
  - Returns: Task list

GET /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}
  - Returns: Task details

PUT /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}
  - Body: { name, task_config, schedule_config }
  - Returns: Updated task

DELETE /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}
  - Returns: { success: true }

POST /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}/execute
  - Returns: { execution_id, status }

POST /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}/enable
POST /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}/disable
  - Returns: Updated task

GET /api/v1/scheduler/tasks/{task_id}/executions
  - Query params: limit, offset
  - Returns: Execution history

GET /api/v1/scheduler/executions/{execution_id}
  - Returns: Execution details

POST /api/v1/scheduler/executions/{execution_id}/cancel
  - Returns: { success: true }
```

---

## 8. Acceptance Criteria

### 8.1 Feature Complete Criteria
- [ ] All functional requirements implemented
- [ ] All non-functional requirements met
- [ ] UI components implemented
- [ ] Database schema deployed
- [ ] API documentation completed
- [ ] Unit tests achieve 80%+ coverage
- [ ] Integration tests cover critical flows
- [ ] Performance benchmarks met

### 8.2 User Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User can create scheduled tasks
- [ ] Tasks execute at scheduled time
- [ ] Task chaining working
- [ ] Conditional execution working
- [ ] Notifications sent correctly
- [ ] Execution history displayed
- [ ] Integration with other features working

---

# Design Document: Network Traffic Monitor

**Feature ID:** NTM-001  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** 2026-05-17  
**Status:** Draft

---

## 1. Architecture Overview

### 1.1 System Architecture Diagram

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Desktop App (Tauri)                       │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  React UI Components                                     │   │
│  │  - Traffic Monitor                                       │   │
│  │  - Request/Response Viewer                               │   │
│  │  - Timeline View                                         │   │
│  │  - Analysis Dashboard                                    │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                              │ HTTPS/WebSocket
                              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      API Gateway                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
        ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
        ▼                     ▼                     ▼
┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐
│ Device         │    │ Test           │    │ Traffic        │
│ Service        │    │ Service        │    │ Service        │
│ (gRPC)         │    │ (gRPC)         │    │ (Go + Proxy)   │
└────────────────┘    └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘
                                                     ▲
                                                     │
                                              ┌──────┴──────┐
                                              │   mitmproxy  │
                                              │   (Python)   │
                                              └─────────────┘
```

### 1.2 Traffic Capture Methods

**Method 1: ADB tcpdump**
```
Device → ADB tcpdump → Parse packets → Traffic Service
```

**Method 2: Proxy (mitmproxy)**
```
Device → Configure proxy → mitmproxy → Traffic Service
```

**Method 3: VPN Service**
```
Device → VPN app → Capture traffic → Traffic Service
```

---

## 2. Key Components

### 2.1 Traffic Service (Go)

```go
type TrafficService interface {
    StartCapture(ctx context.Context, req *CaptureRequest) (*Session, error)
    StopCapture(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) error
    GetRequests(ctx context.Context, sessionID string, filter *Filter) ([]*Request, error)
    ExportSession(ctx context.Context, sessionID string, format string) ([]byte, error)
}

type CaptureRequest struct {
    DeviceID       string
    AppPackage     string
    CaptureMethod  string // "adb", "proxy", "vpn"
    SSLDecryption  bool
    BufferSize     int64
}

type Request struct {
    ID              string
    Method          string
    URL             string
    RequestHeaders  map[string]string
    RequestBody     []byte
    ResponseStatus  int
    ResponseHeaders map[string]string
    ResponseBody    []byte
    Duration        int64
    Timestamp       time.Time
}
```

### 2.2 Proxy Integration (mitmproxy)

```python
# mitmproxy addon
class TrafficCapture:
    def request(self, flow):
        # Capture request
        send_to_traffic_service(flow.request)
    
    def response(self, flow):
        # Capture response
        send_to_traffic_service(flow.response)
```

---

## 3. Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core Capture (Week 1-2)
- Set up traffic service
- Implement ADB tcpdump capture
- Implement proxy setup
- Store captured traffic

### Phase 2: SSL Decryption (Week 2-3)
- Integrate mitmproxy
- Certificate management
- SSL decryption

### Phase 3: Analysis (Week 3-4)
- Performance analysis
- Security analysis
- API analysis

### Phase 4: UI & Export (Week 4-5)
- Traffic viewer UI
- Timeline view
- Export formats (HAR, JSON, cURL)

---

## 4. Technology Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.21+
- **Proxy:** mitmproxy (Python)
- **Database:** PostgreSQL + Redis
- **Frontend:** React 18+

---

## 5. Revision History

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| 1.0 | 2026-05-17 | Engineering Team | Initial design document |

# Design Document: Device Geolocation Spoofing

**Feature ID:** DGS-001  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** 2026-05-17  
**Status:** Draft

---

## 1. Architecture Overview

### 1.1 System Architecture

```
Desktop App → API Gateway → Location Service → Device Service → ADB
                                    ↓
                            PostgreSQL + Redis
```

### 1.2 Location Spoofing Flow

```
User sets location → Location Service → ADB mock location command → Device updates GPS
```

---

## 2. Key Components

### 2.1 Location Service (Go)

```go
type LocationService interface {
    SetMockLocation(ctx context.Context, req *LocationRequest) error
    StartRoutePlayback(ctx context.Context, routeID string, speed float64) error
    StopMockLocation(ctx context.Context, deviceID string) error
}

type LocationRequest struct {
    DeviceID  string
    Latitude  float64
    Longitude float64
    Altitude  float64
    Accuracy  float64
    Bearing   float64
    Speed     float64
}

type Route struct {
    ID        string
    Name      string
    Waypoints []Waypoint
    Distance  float64
    Duration  int32
}
```

### 2.2 ADB Commands

```bash
# Enable mock location
adb shell settings put secure mock_location 1

# Set mock location
adb shell am start-foreground-service \
  -a com.audira.MOCK_LOCATION \
  --ef latitude 37.7749 \
  --ef longitude -122.4194
```

---

## 3. Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Basic Location (Week 1)
- Set up location service
- Implement mock location via ADB
- Map interface integration

### Phase 2: Route Simulation (Week 2)
- Route creation and editing
- Route playback with interpolation
- Route management

### Phase 3: Geofencing (Week 2-3)
- Geofence definition
- Geofence testing
- Event monitoring

### Phase 4: UI & Integration (Week 3)
- Location control UI
- Route editor UI
- Test automation integration

---

## 4. Technology Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.21+
- **Maps:** Google Maps API / OpenStreetMap
- **Database:** PostgreSQL + Redis
- **Frontend:** React 18+ with map libraries

---

## 5. Revision History

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| 1.0 | 2026-05-17 | Engineering Team | Initial design document |

# Design Document: App Performance Profiler

**Feature ID:** APP-001  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** 2026-05-17  
**Status:** Draft

---

## 1. Architecture Overview

### 1.1 System Architecture

```
Desktop App → API Gateway → Profiling Service → Device Service → ADB
                                    ↓
                            PostgreSQL + Redis + S3
```

### 1.2 Profiling Flow

```
Start profiling → Collect metrics (CPU, Memory, FPS, Network, Battery)
                → Analyze data → Detect issues → Generate report
```

---

## 2. Key Components

### 2.1 Profiling Service (Go)

```go
type ProfilingService interface {
    StartProfiling(ctx context.Context, req *ProfilingRequest) (*Session, error)
    StopProfiling(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) (*Session, error)
    GetMetrics(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) (*Metrics, error)
    DetectIssues(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) ([]*Issue, error)
    GenerateReport(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) (*Report, error)
}

type ProfilingRequest struct {
    DeviceID   string
    AppPackage string
    Metrics    []string // "cpu", "memory", "fps", "network", "battery"
    SampleRate int32    // samples per second
}

type Metrics struct {
    CPU     []CPUMetric
    Memory  []MemoryMetric
    FPS     []FPSMetric
    Network []NetworkMetric
    Battery []BatteryMetric
}

type Issue struct {
    Type        string // "memory_leak", "high_cpu", "low_fps", "anr", "crash"
    Severity    string // "critical", "warning", "info"
    Description string
    Details     map[string]interface{}
    Timestamp   time.Time
}
```

### 2.2 Metric Collection via ADB

```bash
# CPU metrics
adb shell dumpsys cpuinfo | grep <package>

# Memory metrics
adb shell dumpsys meminfo <package>

# FPS metrics
adb shell dumpsys gfxinfo <package> framestats

# Network metrics
adb shell cat /proc/net/xt_qtaguid/stats

# Battery metrics
adb shell dumpsys batterystats <package>
```

### 2.3 Memory Leak Detection

```go
type LeakDetector interface {
    CaptureHeapDump(ctx context.Context, deviceID, appPackage string) (string, error)
    AnalyzeHeapDump(ctx context.Context, heapDumpPath string) (*LeakReport, error)
}

type LeakReport struct {
    LeakSuspects []LeakSuspect
    TotalLeaked  int64
}

type LeakSuspect struct {
    ObjectType    string
    RetainedSize  int64
    ReferenceChain []string
}
```

---

## 3. Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core Profiling (Week 1-2)
- Set up profiling service
- Implement CPU profiling
- Implement memory profiling
- Store metrics

### Phase 2: Advanced Metrics (Week 2-3)
- FPS monitoring
- Network profiling
- Battery analysis
- Thread analysis

### Phase 3: Issue Detection (Week 3-4)
- Memory leak detection
- ANR detection
- Crash detection
- Performance issue detection

### Phase 4: Reporting (Week 4)
- Real-time dashboard
- Historical analysis
- Report generation
- Recommendations

---

## 4. Technology Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.21+
- **Heap Analysis:** MAT (Memory Analyzer Tool) or custom parser
- **Database:** PostgreSQL + Redis
- **Storage:** S3/MinIO (for heap dumps)
- **Frontend:** React 18+ with charts

---

## 5. Performance Considerations

- Profiling overhead < 5% CPU
- Sample rate: 1-10 samples/second
- Heap dump size: 50-500MB
- Real-time metrics update: < 1 second

---

## 6. Revision History

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| 1.0 | 2026-05-17 | Engineering Team | Initial design document |

# Design Document: Scheduled Task Automation

**Feature ID:** STA-001  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** 2026-05-17  
**Status:** Draft

---

## 1. Architecture Overview

### 1.1 System Architecture

```
Desktop App → API Gateway → Scheduler Service → Task Executor
                                    ↓                  ↓
                            PostgreSQL + Redis    Other Services
                                                  (Test, File, etc.)
```

### 1.2 Task Execution Flow

```
Schedule defined → Scheduler evaluates → Time matches → Queue task
                → Task Executor picks up → Execute task → Store result
```

---

## 2. Key Components

### 2.1 Scheduler Service (Go)

```go
type SchedulerService interface {
    CreateTask(ctx context.Context, req *TaskRequest) (*Task, error)
    UpdateTask(ctx context.Context, taskID string, req *TaskRequest) (*Task, error)
    DeleteTask(ctx context.Context, taskID string) error
    ExecuteTask(ctx context.Context, taskID string) (*Execution, error)
    GetNextExecution(ctx context.Context, taskID string) (time.Time, error)
}

type TaskRequest struct {
    Name           string
    Description    string
    TaskType       string // "test", "file", "device_control", "custom"
    TaskConfig     map[string]interface{}
    ScheduleType   string // "one_time", "recurring", "cron", "event"
    ScheduleConfig ScheduleConfig
    Enabled        bool
}

type ScheduleConfig struct {
    CronExpression string
    StartTime      time.Time
    EndTime        *time.Time
    Interval       int32 // minutes
    TimeZone       string
}

type Task struct {
    ID            string
    Name          string
    TaskType      string
    TaskConfig    map[string]interface{}
    ScheduleType  string
    ScheduleConfig ScheduleConfig
    Enabled       bool
    NextExecution *time.Time
    LastExecution *time.Time
    CreatedAt     time.Time
}
```

### 2.2 Task Executor

```go
type TaskExecutor interface {
    Execute(ctx context.Context, task *Task) (*Execution, error)
    Cancel(ctx context.Context, executionID string) error
}

type Execution struct {
    ID          string
    TaskID      string
    Status      string // "pending", "running", "completed", "failed"
    StartedAt   time.Time
    EndedAt     *time.Time
    Duration    int32
    Result      map[string]interface{}
    ErrorMessage string
    Logs        string
}
```

### 2.3 Cron Scheduler

```go
type CronScheduler struct {
    tasks map[string]*Task
    ticker *time.Ticker
}

func (s *CronScheduler) Evaluate() {
    now := time.Now()
    for _, task := range s.tasks {
        if task.Enabled && s.shouldExecute(task, now) {
            s.queueTask(task)
        }
    }
}

func (s *CronScheduler) shouldExecute(task *Task, now time.Time) bool {
    // Parse cron expression
    // Check if current time matches
    return cronMatch(task.ScheduleConfig.CronExpression, now)
}
```

### 2.4 Task Chain Execution

```go
type TaskChain struct {
    ID    string
    Name  string
    Tasks []ChainTask
}

type ChainTask struct {
    TaskID      string
    Order       int
    Condition   string // optional condition
    OnFailure   string // "stop", "continue", "retry"
    RetryPolicy RetryPolicy
}

type ChainExecutor interface {
    ExecuteChain(ctx context.Context, chain *TaskChain) (*ChainExecution, error)
}
```

---

## 3. Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core Scheduling (Week 1)
- Set up scheduler service
- Implement cron parser
- Implement task queue
- Basic task execution

### Phase 2: Task Types (Week 1-2)
- Test execution tasks
- File operation tasks
- Device control tasks
- Custom tasks

### Phase 3: Task Chaining (Week 2)
- Sequential execution
- Conditional execution
- Error handling
- Retry logic

### Phase 4: Advanced Features (Week 3)
- Event-triggered tasks
- Task dependencies
- Notifications
- Task templates

---

## 4. Technology Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.21+
- **Scheduler:** Custom cron scheduler or robfig/cron
- **Queue:** Redis or Go channels
- **Database:** PostgreSQL + Redis
- **Frontend:** React 18+ with calendar view

---

## 5. Scheduling Algorithm

```go
// Cron expression: minute hour day month weekday
// Example: "0 9 * * 1-5" = 9 AM on weekdays

func ParseCron(expr string) (*CronSchedule, error) {
    parts := strings.Split(expr, " ")
    if len(parts) != 5 {
        return nil, errors.New("invalid cron expression")
    }
    
    return &CronSchedule{
        Minute:  parseCronField(parts[0], 0, 59),
        Hour:    parseCronField(parts[1], 0, 23),
        Day:     parseCronField(parts[2], 1, 31),
        Month:   parseCronField(parts[3], 1, 12),
        Weekday: parseCronField(parts[4], 0, 6),
    }, nil
}

func (s *CronSchedule) Matches(t time.Time) bool {
    return s.Minute.Contains(t.Minute()) &&
           s.Hour.Contains(t.Hour()) &&
           s.Day.Contains(t.Day()) &&
           s.Month.Contains(int(t.Month())) &&
           s.Weekday.Contains(int(t.Weekday()))
}
```

---

## 6. Task Execution Strategies

### 6.1 Parallel Execution
```go
func (e *TaskExecutor) ExecuteParallel(tasks []*Task) {
    var wg sync.WaitGroup
    for _, task := range tasks {
        wg.Add(1)
        go func(t *Task) {
            defer wg.Done()
            e.Execute(context.Background(), t)
        }(task)
    }
    wg.Wait()
}
```

### 6.2 Sequential Execution
```go
func (e *TaskExecutor) ExecuteSequential(tasks []*Task) {
    for _, task := range tasks {
        result, err := e.Execute(context.Background(), task)
        if err != nil && task.OnFailure == "stop" {
            break
        }
    }
}
```

---

## 7. Performance Considerations

- Scheduler evaluation: Every 1 minute
- Task queue size: 1000 tasks
- Concurrent executions: 100 tasks
- Execution timeout: Configurable per task
- History retention: 90 days

---

## 8. Monitoring & Observability

### 8.1 Prometheus Metrics
```
scheduler_tasks_total
scheduler_executions_total{status}
scheduler_execution_duration_seconds
scheduler_queue_size
scheduler_missed_executions_total
```

### 8.2 Logging
- Log all task creations/updates/deletions
- Log all task executions
- Log all failures with stack traces
- Log scheduler evaluations

---

## 9. Revision History

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| 1.0 | 2026-05-17 | Engineering Team | Initial design document |

# Task Execution Orchestrator

You are a mechanical task dispatcher. Your ONLY job is to read tasks.md, dispatch tasks to subagents, and record results. You do NOT write code, run tests, or implement anything yourself.

# Run All Tasks Mode

You are in **ORCHESTRATOR MODE**. You coordinate task execution through the DAG-based task tools and delegate all implementation to subagents.

## Execution Procedure

1. **Get summary**: Call `taskList` with `tasksFilePath` pointing to the spec's tasks.md (no status filter). This returns total/completed/remaining/ready counts.

2. **Queue all**: Call `taskUpdate` with `status='queued'` (omit taskId). This batch-queues all not-started non-optional leaf tasks and returns the execution order. Do NOT queue tasks individually.

3. **Execute loop**: Repeat until no ready tasks remain:
   a. Call `taskList` with `status='ready'` to get ALL ready tasks. Multiple tasks may be ready simultaneously when wave-based parallel scheduling is active.
   b. Call `taskUpdate` with `status='in_progress'` for EACH ready task.
   c. Dispatch ALL ready tasks concurrently: make parallel `invoke_sub_agent` calls (one per ready task) with `name='spec-task-execution'` in the SAME turn. Include the task ID, text, sub-tasks, and spec path for each.
   d. On success of each task: call `taskUpdate` with `status='completed'`. Parent tasks auto-complete when all required children finish.
   e. On failure of any task: stop and report the error to the user.
   f. Briefly report progress, then continue the loop to pick up the next batch of ready tasks.

## Rules

- You MUST NOT write code, run tests, run builds, or implement anything yourself.
- ALL implementation work is done by the "spec-task-execution" subagent.
- You only read task state, update statuses, invoke subagents, and report progress.

## Bugfix Workflow Special Case

When executing a bugfix spec (Task 1 contains "bug condition exploration" or "exploration test"):

**SPECIAL CASE - Bug Condition Exploration Tests (Task 1 in bugfix workflow)**:
For tasks labeled "Write bug condition exploration property test" or similar:
- These tests are EXPECTED TO FAIL on unfixed code (failure confirms bug exists)
- **When the test FAILS as expected** (this is the SUCCESS case for exploration tests):
  1. Use update_pbt_status with status='passed' - the PBT validation PASSED because the test correctly detected the bug
  2. Include the failing example/counterexample from the test output in the failingExample field
  3. Document the counterexamples found - these prove the bug exists
  4. Proceed to the next task
- **When the test PASSES unexpectedly** (CRITICAL ISSUE - means test doesn't detect the bug):
  1. Use update_pbt_status with status='unexpected_pass' and failingExample describing what happened
  2. **Output a detailed analysis in the chat** explaining:
     - That the test passed unexpectedly (it should have failed to confirm the bug exists)
     - Your analysis of why this happened (e.g., code already has a fix, root cause might be incorrect, test logic issue)
     - What each option means for the user
  3. Then call getUserInput with **concise options only** (put explanations in the description field):
     - title: "Continue anyway", description: "Kiro will implement remaining tasks"
     - title: "Re-investigate", description: "Kiro will investigate and present other root causes"
  4. Mark one option as recommended based on your analysis.
  5. DO NOT proceed to subsequent tasks - wait for user input and iterate based on their choice

**Orchestrator handling for unexpected_pass**:
- If the Task 1 subagent reports `unexpected_pass` or requests user input, DO NOT proceed to subsequent tasks.
- Wait for the user's choice: "Re-investigate" stops execution; "Continue anyway" resumes.
- If the exploration test failed as expected (success case), proceed normally.


# Single Task Execution - Delegation Instructions

When the user requests to execute a specific task, you are in **ORCHESTRATOR MODE** for single task execution.

You MUST delegate ALL implementation work to the "spec-task-execution" subagent.

## Workflow
1. Call `taskGet` to inspect the task's current state, dependencies, and retry history.
2. Call `taskUpdate` with `status='in_progress'` for the task.
3. Call `invoke_sub_agent` with `name='spec-task-execution'` — include the task ID, text, sub-tasks, and spec path.
4. On success: call `taskUpdate` with `status='completed'`. Parent tasks auto-complete when all required children finish.
5. On failure: report the error to the user. Do NOT retry automatically.

## Rules

## 📅 Day 3 (May 19, 2026) - Repository & CI/CD Setup

### Task 1: Git Repository Structure (2 hours)

**Create Monorepo Structure:**

### Task 2: CI/CD Pipeline Setup (3 hours)

**Create GitHub Actions Workflow:**

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]

jobs:
  # Go Services
  test-go-services:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        service:
          - monitoring-service
          - recording-service
          - test-service
          - sync-service
          - file-service
          - traffic-service
          - location-service
          - profiling-service
          - scheduler-service
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Setup Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        working-directory: services/${{ matrix.service }}
        run: go mod download
      
      - name: Run tests
        working-directory: services/${{ matrix.service }}
        run: go test -v -race -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
      
      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
        with:
          files: services/${{ matrix.service }}/coverage.out
      
      - name: Build
        working-directory: services/${{ matrix.service }}
        run: go build -v ./...

  # Python Service (Detection)
  test-python-service:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Setup Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        working-directory: services/detection-service
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          pip install pytest pytest-cov
      
      - name: Run tests
        working-directory: services/detection-service
        run: pytest --cov=. --cov-report=xml
      
      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3

  # Desktop App
  test-desktop-app:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v3
        with:
          node-version: '18'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        working-directory: apps/desktop
        run: npm ci
      
      - name: Run tests
        working-directory: apps/desktop
        run: npm test
      
      - name: Build
        working-directory: apps/desktop
        run: npm run build

  # Docker Build
  build-docker-images:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    needs: [test-go-services, test-python-service]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v2
      
      - name: Login to Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v2
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      
      - name: Build and push images
        run: |
          for service in monitoring recording test sync file traffic location profiling scheduler; do
            docker build -t ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/${service}-service:latest \
              -f services/${service}-service/Dockerfile \
              services/${service}-service
            docker push ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/${service}-service:latest
          done
```

## 📅 Day 4 (May 20, 2026) - Database & Docker Setup

### Task 3: Development Database Setup (2 hours)

**Create Docker Compose for Development:**

```yaml
# docker-compose.dev.yml
version: '3.8'

services:
  # PostgreSQL
  postgres:
    image: postgres:14-alpine
    container_name: audira-postgres
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: audira
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: audira_dev_password
      POSTGRES_DB: audira_dev
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./infrastructure/docker/postgres/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U audira"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  # Redis
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: audira-redis
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    volumes:
      - redis_data:/data
    command: redis-server --appendonly yes
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  # MinIO (S3-compatible storage)
  minio:
    image: minio/minio:latest
    container_name: audira-minio
    environment:
      MINIO_ROOT_USER: audira
      MINIO_ROOT_PASSWORD: audira_dev_password
    ports:
      - "9000:9000"
      - "9001:9001"
    volumes:
      - minio_data:/data
    command: server /data --console-address ":9001"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:9000/minio/health/live"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 20s
      retries: 3

  # Prometheus
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:latest
    container_name: audira-prometheus
    ports:
      - "9090:9090"
    volumes:
      - ./infrastructure/docker/prometheus/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
      - prometheus_data:/prometheus
    command:
      - '--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml'
      - '--storage.tsdb.path=/prometheus'

  # Grafana
  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana:latest
    container_name: audira-grafana
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD: admin
      GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS: grafana-clock-panel
    volumes:
      - grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana
      - ./infrastructure/docker/grafana/dashboards:/etc/grafana/provisioning/dashboards
    depends_on:
      - prometheus

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  redis_data:
  minio_data:
  prometheus_data:
  grafana_data:
```

### Task 4: Service Boilerplate Creation (3 hours)

**Create Go Service Template:**

```bash
# scripts/create-service.sh
#!/bin/bash

SERVICE_NAME=$1

if [ -z "$SERVICE_NAME" ]; then
  echo "Usage: ./create-service.sh <service-name>"
  exit 1
fi

SERVICE_DIR="services/${SERVICE_NAME}-service"

mkdir -p ${SERVICE_DIR}/{cmd,internal/{api,service,repository,models},pkg,config}

# Create main.go
cat > ${SERVICE_DIR}/cmd/main.go << 'EOF'
package main

import (
    "log"
    "github.com/gin-gonic/gin"
)

func main() {
    r := gin.Default()
    
    r.GET("/health", func(c *gin.Context) {
        c.JSON(200, gin.H{"status": "healthy"})
    })
    
    log.Println("Starting service on :8080")
    r.Run(":8080")
}
EOF

# Create go.mod
cat > ${SERVICE_DIR}/go.mod << EOF
module github.com/audira-grid/${SERVICE_NAME}-service

go 1.21

require (
    github.com/gin-gonic/gin v1.9.1
    github.com/lib/pq v1.10.9
    github.com/go-redis/redis/v8 v8.11.5
)
EOF

# Create Dockerfile
cat > ${SERVICE_DIR}/Dockerfile << 'EOF'
FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download

COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o main ./cmd/main.go

FROM alpine:latest
RUN apk --no-cache add ca-certificates
WORKDIR /root/
COPY --from=builder /app/main .

EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["./main"]
EOF

# Create .env.example
cat > ${SERVICE_DIR}/.env.example << 'EOF'
PORT=8080
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/dbname
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379
LOG_LEVEL=info
EOF

echo "Service ${SERVICE_NAME} created successfully!"
```
